Mixture model (Maier)

Source: vignettes/mixture-model-maier.qmd

Introduction

The mixture model approach to publication bias adjustment (Maier et al., 2023, metamix) models the observed distribution of effects as a mixture of two components: studies reporting genuine effects and studies reflecting publication-bias-inflated estimates.

Unlike selection weight models (which reweight existing studies) or PET-PEESE (which adjusts via regression), the mixture model directly represents the two-population structure hypothesised under publication bias: a component of unbiased studies and a component of overestimates that survived the selection filter.

Model specification

Each study ii is assigned latent membership zi∈{0,1}z_i \in \{0, 1\}:

  • zi=0z_i = 0: study draws from the unbiased distribution
  • zi=1z_i = 1: study draws from the biased distribution

The likelihood is

p(yi∣θ,σθ,μb,σb,πb)=(1−πb)⋅𝒩(yi∣θ,σθ2+si2)+πb⋅𝒩(yi∣μb,σb2+si2) p(y_i \mid \theta, \sigma_\theta, \mu_b, \sigma_b, \pi_b) = (1 - \pi_b) \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \theta, \sigma_\theta^2 + s_i^2) + \pi_b \cdot \mathcal{N}(y_i \mid \mu_b, \sigma_b^2 + s_i^2)

where:

  • θ\theta is the true pooled effect (unbiased component mean)
  • σθ\sigma_\theta is the true between-study heterogeneity (unbiased component SD)
  • μb>θ\mu_b > \theta is the mean of the biased component (constrained to exceed the true effect)
  • σb\sigma_b is the SD of the biased component
  • πb\pi_b is the probability that a published study belongs to the biased component

The constraint μb>θ\mu_b > \theta encodes the assumption that publication bias inflates effects upward (positive direction). For negative effects, the constraint is μb<θ\mu_b < \theta.

Priors

θ∼𝒩(0,1),σθ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), \qquad \sigma_\theta \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5)

μb∼𝒩(θ+δb,σb2),δb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \mu_b \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta + \delta_b, \sigma_b^2), \quad \delta_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.5)

σb∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5),πb∼Beta(1,4) \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, 0.5), \qquad \pi_b \sim \text{Beta}(1, 4)

The Beta(1,4)\text{Beta}(1, 4) prior on πb\pi_b assigns prior mass primarily to small proportions of biased studies, encoding the assumption that most published studies are genuine.

Key estimands

  • θ\theta: The bias-corrected pooled effect, estimated from the unbiased component only.
  • πb\pi_b: The posterior probability that a typical published study is drawn from the biased component.
  • Mixture-averaged effect: (1−πb)θ+πbμb(1 - \pi_b)\theta + \pi_b \mu_b — the effect that would be estimated by a naive meta-analysis ignoring the mixture structure.

Fitting the mixture model 

fit_mix <- bayesma(
  data,
  model_type   = "mixture_model",
  p_bias_prior = beta(1, 4)
)

summary(fit_mix)

Interpreting results

The posterior for πb\pi_b quantifies the evidence for a biased subpopulation. A 95% credible interval for πb\pi_b that excludes zero provides evidence for the existence of biased studies. The posterior for θ\theta provides the bias-corrected estimate.

πb\pi_b posterior median Interpretation
<0.05< 0.05 Little evidence of a biased component
0.050.05–0.200.20 Moderate evidence; some inflation likely
>0.20> 0.20 Strong evidence; substantial proportion of biased studies

Comparison with selection models

The mixture model and selection weight models (Vevea-Hedges, Copas) approach the same problem from different angles:

Aspect Mixture model Selection model
Mechanism Latent two-population structure Reweighting by pp-value or precision
Estimand Effect in unbiased component Effect corrected for reweighting
Assumption Biased studies inflate effect magnitude Studies selected with prob w(pi)w(p_i)
kk requirement k≥10k \geq 10 k≥5k \geq 5

When selection is primarily pp-value based, selection models are better theoretically motivated. When the selection mechanism is unknown or involves factors beyond significance, the mixture model is more agnostic.

Prior sensitivity

The mixture model is sensitive to the prior on πb\pi_b. A sensitivity analysis comparing Beta(1,1)\text{Beta}(1, 1) (uniform) and Beta(1,9)\text{Beta}(1, 9) (strong prior towards no bias) is recommended.
